text: The surgeon places a 12mm Hassan port in the right upper quadrant and three 8mm robotic ports, two along the old chevron incision on the left side and one in the epigastric area. Adhesions from prior surgery are lysed to gain exposure. The hernia in the diaphragm is visualized, showing the transverse colon and omentum protruding through the defect. The anterior portion of the hernia sac is dissected using scissors attached to monopolar electrocautery. The diaphragm is opened with a harmonic scalpel to assist in reducing the hernia, causing loss of pneumoperitoneum as air escapes into the pleural space. A diamond flex liver retractor is placed through the assistant port to elevate the diaphragm and improve visualization of the hernia defect.

The surgeon reduces the hernia contents, including the transverse colon and omentum, back into the abdominal cavity through the enlarged defect in the diaphragm. The defect measures approximately five centimeters in length.

The surgeon visualizes the lung through the primary diaphragmatic defect. A secondary defect lateral to the primary one is observed, containing incarcerated omentum, which is reduced. Using a PEDS V-lock suture, the surgeon begins the primary repair of the diaphragmatic defect, starting anteriorly to elevate the defect for better visualization. Imbricated suture bites are taken to strengthen the closure.

The surgeon moves the liver retractor inferiorly to visualize the lower aspect of the diaphragmatic defect. An 8 cm by 5 cm Allomax biological mesh is placed over the closed hernia defect to reinforce the repair, anchored circumferentially using a V-lock suture.
